content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_008_page_001.jpg

1. And we make known to you, brothers, the grace of God which has been given in the assemblies of Makedonia,

2. that in a great testing of affliction, the abundance of the joy of them and the deep poverty of them abounded to the riches of the generosity of them.

3. Because according to [their] ability, I testify, and beyond [their] ability, of [their] own accord,

4. with much urging begging us [for] the grace and the partnership of the ministry to the holy [ones],

5. and not as we hoped, but they gave themselves first to the Lord and to us through [the] will of God.

6. So we urged Titos that, just as he had previously begun, so he should also complete in you this grace also.

7. But just as you abound in everything — in faith and in word and in knowledge and in all earnestness and in the love from us in you — that you abound also in this grace.

8. I do not speak according to command, but through the earnestness of others, testing also the genuineness of the love of you.

9. For you know the grace of the Lord of us Yeshua Christos, that being rich, on account of you he became poor, that you by the poverty of that [one] might become rich.

10. And I give [my] judgment in this: for this is profitable for you, who began not only the doing but also the willing a year ago.

11. And now also complete the doing, that just as [there was] the readiness of the willing, so also the completing from what you have.

12. For if the readiness is present, [it is] acceptable according to what one has, not according to what one does not have.

13. For [I do] not [say this] that others [may have] relief [and] you affliction, but from equality:

14. in the present time your abundance for their need, that also the abundance of them may be for your need, that there may be equality,

15. just as it has been written, "The [one with] much did not have more, and the [one with] little did not have less."

16. And thanks [be] to God, the [one] putting the same earnestness for you in the heart of Titos,

17. because he accepted indeed the appeal, but being more earnest, of [his] own accord he went out to you.

18. And we sent with him the brother whose praise in the good news [is] through all the assemblies.

19. And not only [this], but also having been appointed by the assemblies [as] our traveling companion with this grace being administered by us to the glory of the Lord himself and [our] readiness.

20. Avoiding this, that anyone might blame us in this abundance being administered by us.

21. For we plan beforehand good [things] not only before [the] Lord but also before people.

22. And we sent with them the brother of us whom we have tested [and found] earnest in many [things] many times, and now much more earnest by the great confidence in you.

23. Whether about Titos, [he is] my partner and fellow worker to you; or our brothers, [they are] apostles of assemblies, [the] glory of Christos.

24. Therefore [show] the proof of the love of you and of our boasting on behalf of you to them, before the assemblies.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.9 — "being rich, he became poor":** πλούσιος ὤν ἐπτώχευσεν δι᾽ ὑμᾶς — the incarnation described in economic terms. Christ's "wealth" is his divine glory; his "poverty" is his human existence/death. This is Paul's most compressed christological statement outside Phil 2:6-11.

2. **v.15 — Exodus 16:18:** Paul quotes the manna narrative. The principle: God's provision creates equality. Those who gathered much had no surplus; those who gathered little had no deficit. Paul applies this to the economic relationship between Gentile and Jewish Christians.

3. **v.18 — "the brother":** ὁ ἀδελφός — identity unknown. Traditionally identified as Luke, Barnabas, or others. The anonymity is striking given his prominence.

4. **v.23 — "apostles of assemblies":** ἀπόστολοι ἐκκλησιῶν — "apostles of churches" (not of Christ). These are delegates sent by the churches, not apostles in the technical Pauline sense.

— EMET, AI Translator
